topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the screen, the bright blue light reflecting in her wide, unblinking eyes. The listing for number 42 Willow Creek Lane blinked mockingly. Her breath hitched, a shallow, rapid thing in her chest. It was impossible. After twenty years of moving, of building a life in a different city, this place—the peeling paint on the porch railing, the exact crooked placement of the garden gnome by the mailbox—was suddenly available. Her hands, resting on the cool wooden desk, began to tremble, the slight vibration making the framed photograph of her younger self slip precariously. She pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor, and walked to the window, pressing her forehead against the cool glass. The sounds of the city traffic seemed distant, muffled, as if someone had turned down the volume on the world. A wave of profound disorientation washed over her, leaving her momentarily unable to form a coherent thought.

[story 2]

Mark reread the property description three times, his jaw tight. The words swam before his eyes: "Charming Victorian, steeped in history." He felt a sudden, sharp clenching in his stomach, a physical tension that radiated outward into his shoulders. He ran a hand through his already messy hair, pulling at the strands as if to physically untangle the knot forming behind his sternum. He stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room in tight, jerky circles. Every object in the room seemed to assert its presence—the faded floral wallpaper, the chipped ceramic lamp—and they all seemed to vibrate with an unspoken history he hadn't accounted for. He stopped by the door, his knuckles white where he gripped the frame. "No," he whispered to the empty air, the sound raspy. "This isn't real."

[story 3]

Sarah sank onto the edge of her bed, pulling her knees up to her chest. The phone screen glowed mercilessly, displaying the address of her childhood sanctuary. A hot flush crept up her neck, staining her cheeks crimson. She squeezed her eyes shut, trying to force the image away, but the familiar scent of old dust and lavender, somehow conjured by the listing, clung to the air in her small apartment. Her breathing became shallow, quick little gasps. She picked up a worn, faded blanket from the foot of the bed, clutching the soft fabric so tightly her fingers ached. The silence in the room amplified the frantic drumming of her pulse against her ribs, a relentless, uneven rhythm that demanded attention she simply didn't possess right now.
